item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: Instant Upvote.
item.content:encoded:
Don't care if it's old, I love anomalous items like this.

I would love to know what the result would be if you took a measurement of an item, then put it through 914 at 1:1, and measured it again.

Doesn't matter to me how it was found. As far as I'm concerned, the Foundation is always looking for anomalous items, that it finds them in random places is rather fun.

item.title: Response
item.content:encoded: It doesn't mess up the measurements, it actually measures something else. My best theory is that it measures somethings worth to others, that is why some things that are considered worthless weighed less, while some things that were worth more, like a computer or the memory card for it weighed more. I think the researcher weighed little since he didn't mean much to anyone, and the reason the pills weight went up was because the dclass doubted the doctor most likely and thought it was a trap but when the doctor took one they began to believe him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But tone is what makes a SCP artical. They can be as harsh as they want at long as they don't attack the author personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow… I get that we all have different tastes, but calm down! People criticising the writer's tone etc are acting like they're Charles fucking Dickens. There are plenty of SCPs far less interesting or imaginative than this one (which wasn't bad at all) that still made the cut.

Calm down

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Object: Senior Researcher ████ █████
Reading: -0.502

He's worth less than a ballpoint pen…
Upvoted because it's an interesting idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This my headcanon, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi guys. I know this one is ancient history, but I as well am bothered by the "how did the Foundation find this anyway?" problem. I think there should be something odd about the scale that brings it to our attention. The easiest solution is that it works without batteries, through unknown means. That alone might make it worth testing.

We could also try to hint at its origins - maybe instead of it being a regular-looking lab scale, it's clearly something homemade. It was recovered from the lab of a doctoral candidate in psychology who committed suicide, left behind a cryptic note. I would think something that accurately measures your emotional worth to others could be devastating, if you didn't have much.

I understand the whole point of this SCP is to be mysterious about what it does so you don't want to spell that out in the origin story. But I do think there needs to be some initial weirdness that brings this to our attention and starts us testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Moose from last year is right; this only works if you get the hints in the testing… and then the hints start to bury you with such quantity that it becomes stupidly obvious. Even by "old standards" I cannot imagine how something as sparse as this survived as a mainlist rather than an anomalous item entry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh…Yeah, figuring out how the Foundation figured this out is an interesting question, now that I think about it.

As for what the thing's measuring, yes, I read the spoiler, but my headcannon remains that it measures how much emotional value a thing has - a personally-owned fountain pen would have a lot more value than a random ballpoint pen, a wedding ring would have more value than pretty much anything, a person would have much more emotional desire for a life-adding pill upon watching someone else take it (because jealousy)…and Senior Researcher Blackbox is both less emotionally endearing and puts much less emotional value in his marriage than Doctor Blackbox. Upvoted. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"a steel foot-locker labelled 'SCP-1144'."
This seems a bit superfluous to me. I get that it was edited from just black boxes before, and this is better than that, but I think the reader can assume that the thing in which in SCP is contained will be labeled as such except when otherwise stated.

Other than that, I think I like this, in spite of the question as to how the Foundation figured out it was even an SCP, which is bothering me after reading these comments. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah… this is basically why I can't upvote this. It only works as an SCP if you get the hints, but there's no in-article reason for the Foundation to guess what this is, and if they did, they would say it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How would we have ever found out that this is anomalous in the first place?

"retrieval of SCP-1144 from its home on the third floor of an unowned flat in Brixton, London."

"Hey, there's an unowned residential building in Brixton!"

"Yeah? So?"

"So I broke in and went to the third floor!"

"Uh huh. And?"

"Guess what I found there!"

"A portal to another dimension? Zombies? A crashed spaceship?"

"No, no, and no! It was….. an electronic scale !"

"Uh? Like, one of those ones from a chem lab?"

"Yes! And you'll never guess what happened when I plugged it in and weighed random objects!"

"Okay…. what happened when you plugged it in and tried weighing random objects?"

"IT GIVES COMPLETELY NONSENSICAL RESULTS!!!"

"Holy fucking shit!!! Classify it as Keter and summon the O5 council!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is interesting. The two D-classes gave away what it was measuring for me, and I like that a clever reader can catch on. Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote. I normally like my SCPs creepy and provoking uncomfortable feelings, but knowing what it's measuring, this guy goes squarely into the 'interestingly weird' category.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This! This is precisely my thought on the thing, thank you for putting it into the words I couldn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of measuing an unknown quantity consistently I just don't like what it's measuring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It kept me wondering what it measured, like with the wedding ring measuring extremely high, and a fountain pen measuring higher than a £/50/$ note.
It maintains that "hmmm, but what does it do?" feature to it, which sparks curiosity in reading. Voted up from me, indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lots of tonal problems in the containment procedures (the "Do not"s strike me as off; I've never seen imperatives used before), and a very boring concept that is only slightly less boring when explained. Also, why would the box be designated part of the SCP when it's got anomalous properties of its own? Haha, shut up, old me, you're dumb. :V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1ing for similar reasons. I think the tests could be punched up a bit more to make it a more interesting puzzle to solve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, I got that it was measuring that on my first read-through. I kind of like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have now +1'd this, both because I was right that's a good explanation and there's just enough hints in the article itself to be titillating, and for getting rid of the excessive indestructibility. "Unusually tough" is fine by me, like the IV Sins that were posted, "It's tough, we're pretty sure we could open it, but we'd also PROBABLY completely break it in doing so, so we won't try yet."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, perhaps. I will consider putting it back in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spoiler that out, dude :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that the letter should be kept in, just the indestructible nature of the SCP removed. I'm sure the unusual amino acid structure can still work without that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ OOPS
- Now you know!

I like that, it means the stuff we value (diamonds, gold, money, food) would weigh in higher than something like grease, fat, smoke, etc.

I also like it because 682 would have a negative result.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Save that for another SCP. I think the fact it's so out of context is why I react negatively to it.

(Also, bear in mind, I'm just one guy. Other folks may have other opinions. YMMV.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good you know that. It's better that it's not mentioned in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. I thought it was interesting because of the polypeptide chain. In organic systems in Earth, selenium and sulphur have very similar chemical properties, although selenium is heavier. Selenocysteine and Cysteine are essentially the same molecule, with the sulphur in Cysteine replaced by a selenium for Selenocysteine. I thought it was interesting because Selenocysteine is relatively uncommon in polypeptides on Earth, so it suggested to some extent that this wasn't from Earth. But now it just seems excessive. I will remove it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would anticipate a sudden upturn in positive voting. That's awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
answer
answer

Somewhat correct. It actually takes the sum collective opinions of everyone within a 70 gigametre radius regarding that item, which obviously encompasses Earth. A value for a ball-point pen, for example, has value because there are people in the world who want ball-point pens. However, these people do not care which ball-point pen. If someone saw that pen in a shop, and wanted to buy it, then measuring it at that point would cause its value to increase considerably.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this except for the indestructibility for indestructibility's sake. It's basically combining two weird things that have no real relation to each other, one of which (indestructibility) is really uninteresting. I don't think you need it for any narrative reason, even if it isn't any more resilient than a normal analytical scale, the Foundation isn't going to be too invasive with their testing simply because they don't want to arbitrarily break something they don't understand yet.

Other than that, I like the fact that it's measuring something unknown, but we have enough data to almost guess what it is. (Also, I think it would majorly detract from it if we got any closer to the answer.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm…. Correct me if I'm wrong, but…

A guess
Hide the guess

It measures the currently held value of what observers think the things on the scale are "worth" in some unit of measurement we don't know?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ….. is it measuring how interesting something is? How much a given person is interested in something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem at all. And for the record, I'm really liking where this SCP is going.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much, deValmont. I have replaced the picture with yours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Originally I did not like this idea, but now with the edits, the idea that maybe further testing notes will be added to reveal what it is indeed measuring intrigues me. upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll upload it to the page under the name 1144.jpg, I'll let you check it and change the link if you're happy with it. I see you're linking externally, that is risky because if the picture is changed or removed from the original site it disappears from here too. Just change your hotlink to 1144.jpg instead of the full address of the original picture.

To add a picture to an SCP page locally, click 'Files' at the bottom, and upload it from your computer. This gives you the change to make changes to pictures that aren't quite right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's very kind of you. If it's not too much trouble, that would be great. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's only a little thing, but if you want someone to 'shop out the display numbers on the picture so you don't have to say it's not the actual SCP, I'm more than happy to do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Regarding tone, "the display will flicker into life" is a departure from the generally good and clinical writing of the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote for now, due to minor (but not major) tone issues. Also, it could be shortened. The indestructibility of the object is, I gather, not intended to be its interesting feature so I'd suggest removing the indestructibility entirely as it's distracting and makes the article longer than it needs to be.

Shorten the test logs so that it looks more like a systematic analysis of the object's properties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not certain, but you could just put it in a collapsible. I'm intrigued to know what it's measuring myself. Sentimental value?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With the changes made, and understanding what this object is… I dunno, I think it's kinda nifty. Upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I'll tell you what it is measuring. How do you make it so you have to highlight text to see it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So is it measuring something interesting?

Because so far, all you're showing us is "hard-to-destroy scale that gives nonsensical results". There's nothing interesting here. I might even go so far as to say it's boring.

Seriously, there's nothing wrong with telling us — in the talk page — what the idea is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aww… That's a shame. :( I will need to amend that part, in that case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that the "measuring an unknown quantity" thing is cool. I just disagree with some of the formatting choices, as described below. And yes, collapsibles are your friends in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a common sign of a lazy SCP to use the quality of other SCPs to bolster it up. The best way to look at it is would this still be the same quality if you changed every SCP in it to a different type of dwarf hamster. In this case, I think it would, and so you're not gaining anything by using SCPs, and the researchers wouldn't be learning anything by using the different SCPs either. Maybe replace the SCPs with something that would be more likely to ascertain what the scale is detecting.

Also, and I would consider this a big one for developing this into a good SCP - do YOU know what it's detecting? I would make sure you do, and also make it so a clever reader could work it out too. Experimental logs will be good for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to be able to help with tone but it's something I haven't yet mastered, it's just a feeling in regards to the writing, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1144 currently resides in the fifth storage basement of [DATA EXPUNGED], within a steel foot-locker labelled '█████████'.

"So did we put the SCP in the box labelled 'Kitchen Stuff', the box labelled 'Bedroom Stuff' or the box labelled 'Beware Ye Mortals, Thine End Draws Near'?"

"The bedroom one, I think"

Dr ██████ put in a request to have several SCP specimens from various [DATA EXPUNGED] to be brought in for measurement with SCP-1144. His request was reviewed and accepted; this log documents the observations of the tertiary research team.

Oh, HELL no. The in-universe Foundation actively discourages cross-testing because you don't know whether or not these unexplained thingies might combine to destroy the universe. They especially wouldn't be testing it with known dangerous SCPs like 173 & 106.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. That was the idea. :D

And in case you're wondering, I do know what SCP-1144 is measuring. But I'm not going to say. :O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do we know it's measuring something rather than just being messed up? we have no idea what it measures or by what methods it measures the unknown thing. So look at this from a discovery angle, it's a scale that measures 'nothing'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for taking the time to look at my creation. Could you be more specific about the problems you have with the tone? I was concerned about mentioning other SCPs, but I figured by measuring them, it was hardly interfering, and thus would have been acceptable. This is my first SCP after all. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But (as far as I can see) it's not fucking up. It's just measuring something we're not seeing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like it, though you should probably look up how to use collapsibles and put the experiments in them to shorten the page's length a bit. I like the idea of a scale that weighs a completely inscrutable property, and does so consistently; it knows what it's doing, but we don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like this one but it suffers a couple new member mistakes, it has trouble with tone (to me) and it drags on a bit, you also mention several SCPs which is a no-no. I also do not understand what is so special about this aside from it's durability, this seems like the kind of thing that would make the Anomalous Objects page (Invulnerable scale that fucks up when it measures.)

*With the new changes/improvements it is much better, enough to deserve an upvote even if I still have no idea what it's measuring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have upvoted, under normal circumstances I would probably have held back on this one until you've had a bit of feedback and done a few improvements, but I think it's harsh that because you ballsed up with the posting that you're starting on -3.

Edit: Oh, and another thing, is there any reason related to its function that it is totally indestructible? If not, and I can't see one, I would probably remove that bit. It's just weird for weird's sake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. I didn't know that would happen w/ wikidot. But yeah, take this to a sandbox for testing purposes.
